title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
description:
A **trie** (pronounced as "try") or **prefix tree** is a tree data structure used to efficiently store and retrieve keys in a dataset of strings. There are various applications of this data structure, such as autocomplete and spellchecker.

Implement the Trie class:

- `Trie()` Initializes the trie object.
- `void insert(String word)` Inserts the string `word` into the trie.
- `boolean search(String word)` Returns `true` if the string `word` is in the trie (i.e., was inserted before), and `false` otherwise.
- `boolean startsWith(String prefix)` Returns `true` if there is a previously inserted string `word` that has the prefix `prefix`, and `false` otherwise.
whatShouldHappen: Use a nested object or a Map for children of each Trie node
Track the end of a word with a boolean flag at each node
Efficiently traverse down the tree character by character for all operations

rows.Node Path: root -> a -> p -> p -> l -> e
root -> a -> p -> p -> l -> e

skeletonHint: Each node in the Trie should have a `children` property (an object mapping characters to nodes) and an `isEndOfWord` boolean. To insert or search, iterate through the string and move from node to node based on characters.
patternExplanation: A Trie is optimal for prefix-based searches as it shares common prefixes among strings, reducing space and time complexity for operations like 'startsWith'.

title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
description:
Design a data structure that supports adding new words and finding if a string matches any previously added string.

Implement the `WordDictionary` class:

- `WordDictionary()` Initializes the object.
- `void addWord(word)` Adds `word` to the data structure, it can be matched later.
- `bool search(word)` Returns `true` if there is any string in the data structure that matches `word` or `false` otherwise. `word` may contain dots `'.'` where dots can be matched with any letter.
whatShouldHappen: Insert words into a Trie structure
Use recursion (DFS) in the search function to handle the wildcard character '.'
Return true if any path matches the word length and characters (or wildcards)
skeletonHint: Use a Trie for storage. For the search, if you encounter a '.', you need to check all children of the current node recursively. If it's a regular character, just follow that character's path.
patternExplanation: A Trie allows for efficient word storage, and DFS is necessary to explore all possible paths when a wildcard character '.' is encountered.

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
description:
Given the `root` of a binary tree, return *its maximum depth*.

A binary tree's **maximum depth** is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.
whatShouldHappen: Use recursion to find the depth of left and right subtrees
Return 0 for null nodes (base case)
Return 1 + maximum of left and right subtree depths
skeletonHint: Think recursively. The maximum depth of a tree is 1 plus the maximum of the depths of its left and right subtrees. What's the base case when the root is null?
patternExplanation: Depth-First Search naturally explores each path to its leaf, making it ideal for calculating height/depth.

title: Same Tree
description:
Given the roots of two binary trees `p` and `q`, write a function to check if they are the same or not.

Two binary trees are considered the same if they are structurally identical, and the nodes have the same value.
whatShouldHappen: Check if both nodes are null (true)
Check if one node is null and the other isn't (false)
Check if values match and recurse for left and right children
skeletonHint: Compare the current nodes `p` and `q`. If both are null, they're the same. If only one is null or their values differ, they're not. Otherwise, recursively check their left and right subtrees.
patternExplanation: Recursively comparing tree structures is a classic application of Depth-First Search.

title: Invert Binary Tree
description: Given the `root` of a binary tree, invert the tree, and return *its root*.
whatShouldHappen: Swap the left and right children of the current node
Recursively invert the left and right subtrees
Return the root after inversion is complete
skeletonHint: To invert a tree, you need to swap the left and right children of every node. This can be done recursively: swap current node's children, then call the same function for both children.
patternExplanation: Inverting a tree is a structural change that can be naturally expressed by visiting each node (DFS) and swapping its subtrees.

title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
description:
A **path** in a binary tree is a sequence of nodes where each pair of adjacent nodes in the sequence has an edge connecting them. A node can only appear in the sequence **at most once**. Note that the path does not need to pass through the root.

The **path sum** of a path is the sum of the node's values in the path.

Given the `root` of a binary tree, return *the maximum path sum of any non-empty path*.
whatShouldHappen: Compute maximum gain from left and right subtrees recursively
Update a global maximum with the sum of current node value + left gain + right gain
Return node value + max(left gain, right gain) to the parent caller
skeletonHint: Use a helper function to return the maximum 'gain' a node can contribute to its parent (node.val + max(leftGain, rightGain)). Inside this helper, update a global variable with the sum of the current node and both its children's gains (a path through this node).
patternExplanation: Finding the maximum path sum requires exploring all paths. DFS allows us to calculate the contribution of each subtree (gain) and evaluate the best path passing through each node.

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
description: Given the `root` of a binary tree, return *the level order traversal of its nodes' values*. (i.e., from left to right, level by level).
whatShouldHappen: Use a queue to process nodes level by level (BFS)
For each level, record the number of nodes and process them as a group
Push children of each node into the queue for the next level
skeletonHint: Breadth-First Search (BFS) is the standard way to traverse a tree level by level. Use a queue. To group values by level, capture the queue size at the start of each level's processing.
patternExplanation: Level-order traversal is a direct application of Breadth-First Search, which visits all nodes at the current depth before moving to the next.

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
description:
Serialization is the process of converting a data structure or object into a sequence of bits so that it can be stored in a file or memory buffer, or transmitted across a network connection link to be reconstructed later in the same or another computer environment.

Design an algorithm to serialize and deserialize a binary tree. There is no restriction on how your serialization/deserialization algorithm should work. You just need to ensure that a binary tree can be serialized to a string and this string can be deserialized to the original tree structure.
whatShouldHappen: Implement `serialize` to convert a tree into a single string (e.g., using DFS with 'null' markers)
Implement `deserialize` to reconstruct the tree from that string
Ensure the round-trip `deserialize(serialize(root))` results in the original tree
skeletonHint: Use pre-order traversal (DFS) for both. For serialization, use a special character or 'null' to represent empty nodes. For deserialization, use a pointer or shift values from your serialized list as you recursively build the tree.
patternExplanation: DFS (specifically pre-order) is very effective for serializing trees because it maintains the structure of the tree in a linear format that can be easily reconstructed.

title: Subtree of Another Tree
description:
Given the roots of two binary trees `root` and `subRoot`, return `true` if there is a subtree of `root` that has the same structure and node values of `subRoot` and `false` otherwise.

A subtree of a binary tree `tree` is a tree that consists of a node in `tree` and all of this node's descendants. The tree `tree` could also be considered as a subtree of itself.
whatShouldHappen: Implement a helper function `isSameTree` to check if two trees are identical
Recursively check if `subRoot` matches the current `root` or any of its subtrees
Return false if the main `root` becomes null without finding a match
skeletonHint: This problem requires checking two things: if the current tree starting at `root` is the same as `subRoot`, OR if `subRoot` is a subtree of `root.left` or `root.right`. Use the `isSameTree` logic as a helper.
patternExplanation: Recursion allows us to check the identity condition at each node of the main tree, effectively searching for the subtree.

title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
description: Given two integer arrays `preorder` and `inorder` where `preorder` is the preorder traversal of a binary tree and `inorder` is the inorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.
whatShouldHappen: Use the first element of `preorder` as the root node
Find the root's index in `inorder` to split the tree into left and right subtrees
Recursively build left and right subtrees by slicing the arrays
skeletonHint: In `preorder`, the first element is always the root. In `inorder`, elements to the left of the root value belong to the left subtree, and elements to the right belong to the right subtree. Use this to recursively split the arrays.
patternExplanation: The structural relationship between preorder and inorder traversals naturally leads to a recursive solution where we divide the problem into smaller sub-trees.

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
description:
Given the `root` of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST).

A **valid BST** is defined as follows:
- The left subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys **less than** the node's key.
- The right subtree of a node contains only nodes with it keys **greater than** the node's key.
- Both the left and right subtrees must also be binary search trees.
whatShouldHappen: Check if every node value is within a valid range (min, max)
Update the range recursively as you traverse down the tree
Return false if any node violates the BST property
skeletonHint: A common mistake is only checking if a node's value is between its direct left and right children. Instead, you must ensure all nodes in the left subtree are less than the current node, and all in the right are greater. Use a helper function with `min` and `max` bounds.
patternExplanation: BST validation requires ensuring that every node satisfies the global constraints inherited from its ancestors, which is naturally handled by passing bounds down during a Depth-First Search.

title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
description: Given the `root` of a binary search tree, and an integer `k`, return *the* `kth` *smallest value (**1-indexed**) of all the values of the nodes in the tree*.
whatShouldHappen: Perform an inorder traversal of the BST
Track the number of nodes visited
Return the value of the kth node visited
skeletonHint: Inorder traversal of a BST visits nodes in ascending order. You can either use recursion to build an array and return the (k-1)th element, or use an iterative approach with a stack to stop exactly at the kth element.
patternExplanation: Inorder traversal of a BST (Left-Root-Right) yields node values in strictly increasing order, making it the most efficient way to find the kth smallest element.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
description:
Given a binary search tree (BST), find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) node of two given nodes in the BST.

According to the [definition of LCA on Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lowest_common_ancestor): “The lowest common ancestor is defined between two nodes `p` and `q` as the lowest node in `T` that has both `p` and `q` as descendants (where we allow **a node to be a descendant of itself**).”
whatShouldHappen: Compare p and q values with the current root value
If both p and q are smaller than root, move to the left subtree
If both p and q are larger than root, move to the right subtree
Otherwise, the current root is the LCA
skeletonHint: Leverage the BST property! If p and q are both on one side of the current node, the LCA must be in that subtree. If they are on opposite sides, the current node is their lowest common ancestor.
patternExplanation: The BST property allows us to determine if both nodes reside in the left or right subtree without a full search. The first node we encounter that 'splits' the two values (or matches one of them) is guaranteed to be the LCA.
